name: Roman Road and Camino Real Loop
name_original: Bárcena de Pie de Concha - Pesquera – circular por la Calzada Romana y Camino Real
description: Hike a moderate 10.0-mile (16.0 km) circular route through historic Roman and Royal Roads, forests, and the Besaya valley.
region_intro: Hiking around Bárcena De Pie De Concha offers diverse landscapes, from river gorges to natural parks and historical routes. The region is characterized by the Besaya River carving through the "Hoces de Bárcena" (gorges), and the presence of the Saja-Besaya Natural Park with its Atlantic forests. Ancient Roman roads and historical trade paths provide a network of trails through varied terrain, including oak groves, chestnut trees, and beech forests. Higher elevations like Pico Jano offer more challenging ascents and panoramic views. Best hiking trails around Bárcena De Pie De Concha The most popular hiking route is Fuente Clara Trail, a 4.3 miles (7.0 km) trail that takes 1 hour 53 minutes to complete. This path leads through Saja-Besaya Natural Park with gentle elevation. Another top favourite among local hikers is Roman Road and Camino Real Loop, a moderate 9.9 miles (16.0 km) path. This route combines ancient Roman paths with historical trade routes through varied landscapes. Local hikers also love the Pico Jano loop from Bárcena, a 11.6 miles (18.7 km) trail leading through the ascent to Pico Jano, offering panoramic views, often completed in about 6 hours 24 minutes. Hiking around Bárcena De Pie De Concha is defined by ancient Roman roads, river gorges, and dense Atlantic forests. The network offers options for various fitness levels. The routes in Bárcena De Pie De Concha are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.7 stars from more than 60 reviews. More than 300 hikers have used komoot to explore Bárcena De Pie De Concha's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many hiking trails are available in Bárcena De Pie De Concha?
A: There are over 30 hiking trails around Bárcena De Pie De Concha, offering a diverse range of experiences from easy strolls to challenging mountain ascents. The komoot community has rated these routes highly, with an average score of 4.7 stars.

Q: Are there any easy, family-friendly hiking trails in the area?
A: Yes, Bárcena De Pie De Concha offers several easy trails suitable for families. A great option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e997033643/fuente-clara-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Fuente Clara Trail&lt;/a&gt;, a 7 km path through Saja-Besaya Natural Park with gentle elevation. Another easy choice is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20501376" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Bárcena Mayor – Old Washhouse of Bárcena Mayor loop&lt;/a&gt;, which is under 4 km and takes about an hour to complete.

Q: What are some of the historical routes I can explore?
A: The region is rich in historical paths. You can hike sections of the ancient Roman Road (Calzada Romana), which is a Site of Cultural Interest. Another significant route is the Camino Real de Las Hoces, an 18th-century royal road. These paths offer a unique blend of natural beauty and historical infrastructure.

Q: Are there any circular hiking routes in Bárcena De Pie De Concha?
A: Yes, there are several circular routes. For a moderate challenge, consider the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e996597986/roman-road-and-camino-real-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Roman Road and Camino Real Loop&lt;/a&gt;, which is 16 km and combines both historical paths through varied landscapes. This route offers a comprehensive experience of the area's heritage and natural beauty.

Q: Where can I find panoramic views during a hike?
A: For rewarding panoramic views, the ascent to Pico Jano is highly recommended. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/14991556" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pico Jano loop from Bárcena&lt;/a&gt; is a challenging 18.7 km trail that leads to the summit at 1,286 meters, offering extensive vistas of the surrounding Cantabrian landscape.

Q: Are there any trails that pass by waterfalls or rivers?
A: Yes, the Besaya River carves through the municipality, creating the dramatic "Hoces de Bárcena" gorges. The Torina River, a tributary, also runs through the area and features a recreational spot with waterfalls near an old electrical plant, providing a scenic fluvial path. You can also find the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4629697" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pool area in the Argonza River&lt;/a&gt; as a highlight.

Q: What is the best time of year to go hiking in Bárcena De Pie De Concha?
A: The region is beautiful year-round, but autumn is particularly stunning. The Atlantic forests, with their oak groves, chestnut trees, and beech forests, transform into a 'festival of colors,' making it an ideal time for vibrant scenery. Spring also offers pleasant temperatures and lush greenery.

Q: Are there any dog-friendly hiking trails or specific rules for dogs?
A: Many trails in Bárcena De Pie De Concha and the Saja-Besaya Natural Park are suitable for dogs. However, it's always recommended to keep dogs on a leash, especially within the Natural Park, to protect wildlife and ensure a pleasant experience for all hikers. Always check local signage for specific regulations.

Q: What kind of natural features can I expect to see in Saja-Besaya Natural Park?
A: Saja-Besaya Natural Park is known for its rich Atlantic forests, including ancient oak groves, chestnut trees, and extensive beech forests. You'll also encounter diverse flora and fauna, and the park's varied terrain offers a true immersion in Cantabria's natural beauty. Don't miss the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4628486" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Beautiful section through the beech forest&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there any moderate trails for experienced hikers looking for a good workout?
A: Absolutely. For a moderate challenge, consider the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/24639610" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Hayedo de Montabliz – Mediaconcha Abandoned Village loop from Pesquera&lt;/a&gt;. This 10.8 km route involves a good elevation gain and takes you through varied terrain, including a beautiful beech forest, offering a rewarding experience for those seeking a more strenuous hike.

Q: What do other hikers enjoy most about the trails here?
A: The komoot community rates the trails in Bárcena De Pie De Concha very highly, with an average score of 4.7 stars from over 60 reviews. Hikers often praise the region's unique combination of ancient historical routes, the dramatic river gorges, and the stunning, dense Atlantic forests. The variety of routes catering to different fitness levels is also frequently highlighted.

Q: Are there any interesting viewpoints or rest areas along the trails?
A: Yes, you can find several points of interest. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4566932" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Moral Plateau&lt;/a&gt; offers great views, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4008075" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Peña Pea Rest Area&lt;/a&gt; provides a convenient spot to take a break and enjoy the surroundings during your hike.
tips:
Located in Bárcena de Pie de Concha and next to the cemetery of this town, is this small church built in the 16th century to replace another temple from the 13th century from which some elements were used, and renovated in the 18th century.

Inside the temple there is a Romanesque baptismal font, decorated with a rope motif on the upper face and two small sketches on the upper part of the vase.

Road that starts from the town of Bárcena de Pie de Concha, stops at Mediaconcha (a town in the middle of the road, now abandoned) and ends in the village of Somaconcha, at the top of the hill that separates the two valleys.
It is believed that it was part of the old road that communicated Portus Blendium (Suances area) with Segisamo (Sasamón, in Burgos) at the time of the ancient Roman Empire.

The Town Hall Building was built between 1752 and 1761 to house both the town hall and the jail. It has a small sundial in one of the ashlars and on one of its lintels there is a sign of "Prison", on the main lintel appears the sign of "Town Hall and the year 1856" and it is believed that on this date it was would make some repair to the building. Currently it also has a plaque dedicated to one of the illustrious sons of the town, the singer of regional tunes D. Aureliano Ruiz.
The Roll of Justice (also called Picota) that is located in the square in front of the Town Hall, was the indication that the town had full jurisdiction and justice could be applied there without the nobility intervening and sometimes it was used as a pillory exposing the head there of the executed. In this case the roll of justice is quite simple, fulfilling its function, since they can generally be quite decorated (although this is not the case).

This was the place where the pledged cattle were collected or guarded until their owner came to claim them.
According to the dictionary of the RAE, the Corral del Prendado was "El corral del Común": In some places of Cantabria and León, a place where the pledged cattle were kept and guarded until their owners claimed them, for having crossed the terms or lines within which they were allowed to graze, or to have entered communal lands or private property.
They were regulated by municipal ordinances, and the owners, in order for them to be delivered, had to pay the corresponding fine for lack of care.

The Camino Real de las Hoces (between Pesquera and Bárcena de Pie de Concha) is a section of the “Camino Real de Reinosa a Santander”, planned to facilitate the movement of cereals and wool from the Castilian plateau to the port of Santander.
The path is part of a more ambitious plan developed in the 18th century by the Marqués de la Ensenada, an enlightened politician serving Fernando VI, and consisted of building a network of roads and navigable canals to improve communications and facilitate the formation of a national market for agricultural products. The works were carried out between 1749 and 1753, becoming the main means of communication and trade between the Meseta and the Cantabrian Sea, until the arrival of the railway in 1866.
Beautiful construction in this small town of Bárcena de Pie de Concha. It is a Catholic temple dating from the twelfth century.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access the trail?
faqs.answer: While specific parking locations aren't detailed, both Bárcena de Pie de Concha and Pesquera are good starting points for this circular route. You should be able to find parking within or near these villages to begin your hike.

faqs.question: How difficult is this hike?
faqs.answer: This trail is generally considered to be of moderate difficulty, requiring good physical condition. Some sections, particularly the ascent from Pesquera towards Somaconcha, can have notable inclines reaching about 10%. The total elevation gain can be around 400 to over 600 meters depending on the specific path taken.

faqs.question: What historical sites and natural features will I see along the way?
faqs.answer: This route is rich in history and natural beauty. You'll traverse well-preserved sections of the ancient Roman Road from Bárcena de Pie de Concha to Somaconcha and parts of the Camino Real de las Hoces. Along the way, you can see the Church of Santa María de Roimbre, the Town Hall and Justice Pillar of Pesquera, and the Corral del Prendao. The trail also passes through abandoned villages like Mediaconcha and Somaconcha, offering evocative historical elements, and the Church of San Cosme and San Damián. Nature-wise, expect scenic views of the Besaya valley, forested areas with hazels, chestnuts, and oaks, and the impressive viaduct of Montabliz.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to hike this route?
faqs.answer: The trail can be enjoyed during most seasons, but spring and autumn generally offer the most pleasant hiking conditions with milder temperatures and beautiful foliage. Summer can be warm, while winter might bring colder weather and potentially more challenging conditions on some sections.

faqs.question: Is this trail dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: Yes, this trail is generally dog-friendly. However, it's always recommended to keep your dog on a leash, especially when passing through villages, near livestock, or on busier sections of the path, to ensure the safety and comfort of everyone.

faqs.question: Do I need a permit or pay an entrance fee for this hike?
faqs.answer: No, you do not need a permit or have to pay an entrance fee to hike this trail. It is freely accessible to the public.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on this hike?
faqs.answer: The route features varied terrain, including footpaths, paved and unpaved tracks, and some asphalt sections. You'll walk through forested and mountainous areas, including a 'monte' and a 'bosque' with hazels, chestnuts, and oaks.

faqs.question: How long does it take to complete this circular route?
faqs.answer: While the Komoot tour suggests a duration of approximately 4 hours and 47 minutes for the 16 km route, other accounts indicate variations. Some shorter versions are around 8.5 km and take about 3 hours, while longer ones of about 15.1 km might take 3.5 to 4 hours, allowing time to enjoy the path. The Roman Road section itself is roughly 5 km and can take 2 to 3 hours.

faqs.question: Is this a family-friendly hike?
faqs.answer: Given its moderate difficulty and varied terrain, this hike is suitable for families with older children who are accustomed to longer walks and some inclines. Younger children or those less experienced with hiking might find some sections challenging. Consider the shorter variations if hiking with a family.

faqs.question: Are there places to eat or stay near the trail?
faqs.answer: Yes, both Bárcena de Pie de Concha and Pesquera, the villages at the start/end of the circular route, offer options for refreshments and accommodation. You can find cafes, pubs, and restaurants in these towns to refuel after your hike.

faqs.question: Does the trail intersect other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route also passes through sections of the Camino del Besaya: Cantabria (covering 53% of the route) and the Senda de la Reserva del Saja (covering 12% of the route).

faqs.question: What should I bring for this hike?
faqs.answer: For this moderate hike, it's advisable to bring sturdy hiking boots, sufficient water, snacks, sun protection (hat, sunscreen), and layers of clothing suitable for changing weather conditions. A map or GPS device is also recommended, especially for navigating varied terrain.
